turns.content: I have a BME280 sensor and an Arduino Uno. How do I wire it up and read temperature data?

If your BME280 breakout supports I2C (most do), that’s the easiest way to wire it to an Arduino Uno. Wiring (I2C): - BME280 VIN/VCC -> Arduino 3.3V (preferred) or 5V only if your breakout explicitly supports it - BME280 GND -> Arduino GND - BME280 SCL -> Arduino A5 (SCL) - BME280 SDA -> Arduino A4 (SDA) Notes: - The BME280 chip is 3.3V logic; many breakouts include a regulator + level shifting so they can be used on 5V boards. If yours is a bare module without level shifting, use 3.3V and a level shifter for SDA/SCL. - I2C address is commonly 0x76 or 0x77 depending on a solder jumper or SDO pin. Software (using Adafruit library): 1) Install libraries in Arduino IDE: - Adafruit BME280 Library - Adafruit Unified Sensor 2) Example sketch: ```cpp #include <Wire.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} if (!bme.begin(0x76)) { // try 0x77 if this fails Serial.println("Could not find a valid BME280 sensor, check wiring/address!"); while (1); } } void loop() { float t = bme.readTemperature(); // °C Serial.print("Temp: "); Serial.print(t); Serial.println(" *C"); delay(1000); } ``` If you tell me which exact BME280 board you have (pins labeled VIN/3V3, SDO, CS, etc.) I can confirm whether you should power it from 3.3V or 5V and which address to use.
The board has pins labeled VIN, GND, SCL, SDA, CSB, SDO. No 3V3 pin. Does that mean it’s safe on 5V?

That labeling usually indicates a breakout with an onboard regulator (VIN) and often level shifting for I2C/SPI lines, but not always. “VIN” strongly suggests you can feed it 3–5V (sometimes up to 6V), and it will regulate down to 3.3V for the sensor. The missing “3V3” pin doesn’t guarantee level shifting though—it only guarantees a regulator is likely present. How to sanity-check before using 5V logic: 1) Look for these parts on the board: - A small 3.3V regulator (often marked “662K”, “AMS1117-3.3”, “ME6211”, etc.) - Level shifters / MOSFETs or a chip like BSS138 arrays, or resistor networks near SDA/SCL 2) If the board is tiny with only the BME280 and maybe one regulator, it might not shift logic. 3) If you have a multimeter: - Power VIN with 5V, measure voltage on the BME280 VDD pin (not always accessible) or on a 3.3V test pad if present. If it’s ~3.3V, regulator exists. - For logic shifting: harder to test quickly, but many “VIN” boards intended for Arduino have it. Conservative approach: - Power from 3.3V on the Uno and keep I2C pullups to 3.3V. But the Uno’s I2C pins already have pullups? Not internal by default; many breakouts include pullups to their VCC/VIN side. - If you power the breakout from 3.3V (Uno 3.3V pin), the I2C pullups on the breakout (if present) will pull SDA/SCL to 3.3V, which is safe. Recommended wiring for safety: - VIN -> Uno 3.3V - GND -> GND - SCL -> A5 - SDA -> A4 Leave CSB and SDO alone for I2C (but see address below). I2C address selection: - SDO sets the address: typically - SDO to GND => 0x76 - SDO to VCC => 0x77 If you leave SDO floating, it may be unpredictable; many boards have a default pull resistor. If you’re unsure, you can run an I2C scanner to find the address. Do you want an I2C scanner sketch to confirm the address? That’s the fastest way to know you’re talking to the sensor.
Yes, give me the I2C scanner. Also, what do I do with CSB and SDO if I’m using I2C?

Here’s a standard I2C scanner sketch. Upload it, open Serial Monitor at 9600, and it will list detected I2C device addresses. ```cpp #include <Wire.h> void setup() { Wire.begin(); Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} Serial.println("I2C Scanner"); } void loop() { byte error, address; int nDevices = 0; Serial.println("Scanning..."); for (address = 1; address < 127; address++) { Wire.beginTransmission(address); error = Wire.endTransmission(); if (error == 0) { Serial.print("I2C device found at 0x"); if (address < 16) Serial.print("0"); Serial.println(address, HEX); nDevices++; } } if (nDevices == 0) Serial.println("No I2C devices found"); else Serial.println("Done"); delay(2000); } ``` What to do with CSB and SDO for I2C: - CSB (Chip Select, active low) must be pulled HIGH for I2C mode on BME280. Many breakout boards already pull it up. If yours doesn’t, connect CSB to VIN (or 3.3V if powering at 3.3V). If left floating, it can cause issues. - SDO selects the I2C address: - SDO to GND => address 0x76 - SDO to VCC => address 0x77 If your scanner finds the chip, you can leave SDO as-is. If nothing is found and wiring is correct, try explicitly wiring: - CSB -> VIN - SDO -> GND (for 0x76) then scan again Also make sure you have: - SDA -> A4 - SCL -> A5 - Common GND If the scanner shows nothing, tell me: - What voltage you used on VIN (3.3V or 5V) - Whether you tied CSB high - Any other I2C devices on the bus
The scanner finds a device at 0x76. Great. Now I want to build a weather station that logs temp, humidity, pressure, and maybe altitude. How should I structure the code?

A clean beginner-friendly structure is to separate: initialization, sensor reading, data formatting, and logging/output. You can do it in a single sketch but keep it modular with functions and a simple “sample interval” timer. Key pieces: 1) Global config: sample period, sea-level pressure for altitude, serial baud. 2) Setup: initialize Serial, Wire, sensor. 3) Loop: if it’s time to sample, read sensor values, compute derived values (altitude), print/log. Example skeleton: ```cpp #include <Wire.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; const uint32_t SAMPLE_MS = 2000; uint32_t lastSample = 0; // Sea-level pressure in hPa for altitude calc. // 1013.25 is “standard”; real value varies. float seaLevel_hPa = 1013.25; bool initBME() { if (!bme.begin(0x76)) return false; // Optional: set sampling (oversampling/filter) if you want // bme.setSampling(...); return true; } void readBME(float &tC, float &rh, float &p_hPa, float &alt_m) { tC = bme.readTemperature(); rh = bme.readHumidity(); p_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; // Pa -> hPa alt_m = bme.readAltitude(seaLevel_hPa); } void printCSV(uint32_t ms, float tC, float rh, float p_hPa, float alt_m) { Serial.print(ms); Serial.print(","); Serial.print(tC, 2); Serial.print(","); Serial.print(rh, 2); Serial.print(","); Serial.print(p_hPa, 2); Serial.print(","); Serial.println(alt_m, 2); } void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); while (!Serial) {} Wire.begin(); if (!initBME()) { Serial.println("BME280 not found!"); while (1); } Serial.println("ms,tempC,humRH,presshPa,altm"); } void loop() { uint32_t now = millis(); if (now - lastSample >= SAMPLE_MS) { lastSample = now; float tC, rh, p_hPa, alt_m; readBME(tC, rh, p_hPa, alt_m); printCSV(now, tC, rh, p_hPa, alt_m); } } ``` Why CSV: easy to paste into a spreadsheet or plotter later. About altitude: BME280 altitude is computed from pressure and your assumed sea-level pressure. For “weather station,” altitude is usually less important than pressure trends. If you want a stable altitude readout, you must calibrate seaLevel_hPa or instead compute sea-level pressure given a known altitude. Next step questions: - Do you want to log to an SD card, or just stream over Serial to a computer? - Do you want a display (like 16x2 LCD or OLED)? - Do you want timestamps (RTC module) or is millis() enough?
I’d like to add an SD card module and log data every minute. Also I want a simple 16x2 I2C LCD to show current readings.

Good combo: BME280 + I2C LCD share the same I2C bus, and SD card uses SPI. On an Uno, that’s workable. Hardware connections overview: I2C bus (shared): - BME280 SDA -> A4 - BME280 SCL -> A5 - LCD SDA -> A4 - LCD SCL -> A5 - All GNDs together - Power: LCD modules often want 5V; BME280 board depends (use 3.3V if unsure; if it has VIN/regulator you can use 5V, but 3.3V is safest). If you power BME280 at 3.3V and LCD at 5V, ensure I2C pullups aren’t forcing SDA/SCL to 5V. Many I2C LCD backpacks have pullups to 5V. That could be a problem for a 3.3V-only BME280 board. If your BME280 breakout has level shifting, it’s fine. If not, you’ll want to run the whole I2C bus at 3.3V or add a level shifter. - If your BME280 board is “VIN” type and designed for Uno, it likely tolerates 5V I2C, but I can’t promise without seeing it. SPI for SD module (typical): - SD VCC -> 5V (many modules have regulator) or 3.3V (bare module) - SD GND -> GND - SD SCK -> D13 - SD MISO -> D12 - SD MOSI -> D11 - SD CS -> D10 (or another pin you choose; must match code) Important: The Uno uses D10 as hardware SS; even if you use another CS, keep D10 as OUTPUT to keep SPI in master mode. Software structure: - Use a non-blocking timer for 60 seconds (60000 ms). - Each sample: read sensor, update LCD, append a CSV line to SD. - Consider buffering and keeping the file open vs opening each write. For safety against corruption, many beginners open/write/close each sample. It’s slower but fine once per minute. Libraries: - LiquidCrystal_I2C (or hd44780 library) for LCD - SD library for SD card Example sketch (core idea): ```cpp #include <Wire.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> #include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h> #include <SPI.h> #include <SD.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2); // common address 0x27 or 0x3F const int SD_CS = 10; const uint32_t SAMPLE_MS = 60000UL; uint32_t lastSample = 0; float seaLevel_hPa = 1013.25; File logFile; void lcdPrintFloatAt(int col, int row, float val, int width, int prec) { lcd.setCursor(col, row); // crude formatting: print spaces then value to overwrite old chars char buf[17]; dtostrf(val, width, prec, buf); lcd.print(buf); } void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); Wire.begin(); // LCD lcd.init(); lcd.backlight(); lcd.clear(); lcd.setCursor(0,0); lcd.print("Weather logger"); // BME if (!bme.begin(0x76)) { lcd.setCursor(0,1); lcd.print("BME280 fail"); while (1); } // SD pinMode(10, OUTPUT); if (!SD.begin(SD_CS)) { lcd.setCursor(0,1); lcd.print("SD init fail"); while (1); } // Create file / write header logFile = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (logFile) { logFile.println("ms,tempC,humRH,presshPa,altm"); logFile.close(); } lcd.clear(); } void logSample(uint32_t ms, float tC, float rh, float p_hPa, float alt_m) { File f = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { f.print(ms); f.print(","); f.print(tC, 2); f.print(","); f.print(rh, 2); f.print(","); f.print(p_hPa, 2); f.print(","); f.println(alt_m, 2); f.close(); } else { Serial.println("Failed to open log.csv"); } } void updateLCD(float tC, float rh, float p_hPa) { lcd.setCursor(0,0); lcd.print("T:"); lcdPrintFloatAt(2,0,tC,5,1); lcd.print("C H:"); lcdPrintFloatAt(11,0,rh,5,0); lcd.setCursor(0,1); lcd.print("P:"); lcdPrintFloatAt(2,1,p_hPa,7,1); lcd.print("hPa"); } void loop() { uint32_t now = millis(); if (now - lastSample >= SAMPLE_MS) { lastSample = now; float tC = bme.readTemperature(); float rh = bme.readHumidity(); float p_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; float alt_m = bme.readAltitude(seaLevel_hPa); updateLCD(tC, rh, p_hPa); logSample(now, tC, rh, p_hPa, alt_m); Serial.println("Sample logged"); } } ``` Two immediate gotchas to check: 1) LCD I2C address: commonly 0x27 or 0x3F. Run the I2C scanner; it should show both the BME280 and the LCD backpack. 2) I2C voltage/pullups: if the LCD backpack pulls up to 5V and your BME280 can’t handle it, you can get flaky behavior or damage. If you tell me whether your BME280 breakout has level shifting, we can decide if you need an I2C level shifter module.
I ran the scanner and it shows 0x76 and 0x27. So far it seems to work. But sometimes the BME reads 0.00 or NAN after I added the SD module. Any ideas?

Intermittent NAN/0 readings after adding SD often come from power integrity, wiring noise, or bus contention/timing issues. A few targeted checks usually find it. 1) Power supply / voltage dips - SD modules can draw bursts of current during writes. If you’re powering everything from the Uno’s 5V pin via USB, the 3.3V regulator and/or 5V rail can dip briefly. - Symptoms: sensors fail mid-read, I2C returns invalid data, LCD glitches. What to try: - Add a bulk capacitor near the SD module: 100 µF to 470 µF across VCC-GND. - Add a 0.1 µF ceramic decoupling capacitor close to the BME280 VCC-GND if not already on breakout. - Ensure all grounds are solid and short. 2) SPI chip select behavior If the SD module’s CS pin isn’t held HIGH when not in use, it can interfere with SPI bus lines and sometimes inject noise. Also, some SD modules drive MISO when not selected if CS isn’t correct. Fix: - Ensure SD CS pin has a pull-up (some modules include it). In code, set CS as OUTPUT and write HIGH before initializing other SPI devices (even if you have none). - Example: ```cpp pinMode(SD_CS, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(SD_CS, HIGH); SPI.begin(); SD.begin(SD_CS); ``` 3) I2C bus speed / long wires - If wires are longer or messy, I2C can get marginal. SD activity can add EMI. Try: - Keep SDA/SCL wiring short and twisted with GND if possible. - Lower I2C speed (Wire.setClock(100000); default is 100k on Uno, but some cores/libraries may set 400k). 4) Library re-entrancy / timing - The Adafruit BME280 library does I2C transactions; SD does SPI. They shouldn’t conflict directly, but if you have any interrupts or other libraries, timing can get weird. - Make sure you aren’t calling BME reads inside an interrupt (like a timer ISR). Keep reads in loop. 5) Check for memory fragmentation / String usage - If you used Arduino String heavily for CSV lines, heap fragmentation can cause weirdness after a while. Prefer fixed char buffers and print directly to file. 6) Confirm you’re not mixing 3.3V-only sensor with 5V pullups - If the LCD backpack pulls SDA/SCL to 5V and the BME280 breakout is not level shifted, it might “work” but be unreliable, especially with noise. SD writes causing spikes can push it over the edge. - If you can, identify if your BME280 breakout has level shifters. If not, add an I2C level shifter or remove/disable the LCD pullups and add pullups to 3.3V. Quick experiment to isolate: - Comment out SD writes but keep SD wired. If NAN disappears, it’s power/noise during writes. - Then keep SD writes but disconnect LCD. If NAN disappears, it’s I2C voltage/pullup interaction or bus loading. If you can answer: - How are you powering the BME280 (VIN to 5V or 3.3V)? - Is your SD module the common one with a big socket and 6 pins (VCC, GND, MISO, MOSI, SCK, CS), or a bare microSD adapter? - Do the NANs happen exactly when logging (once per minute), or randomly?
BME VIN is on 5V. SD module is the common one with the socket and those 6 pins. NAN seems to happen right when it writes to SD.

That points strongly to a power dip/noise during SD write bursts. Do these in order (each is simple and has a good chance of fixing it): 1) Add bulk capacitance at SD module - Put 220 µF (or 100–470 µF) electrolytic across SD VCC and GND, physically close to the SD module pins. - Also add a 0.1 µF ceramic in parallel if you have one. 2) Improve grounding and wiring - Run a dedicated GND wire from Uno GND to SD GND and another to sensor/LCD ground rather than daisy-chaining through breadboard rails if possible. - Keep SD wires short, especially SCK. 3) Ensure SD CS is high except during access In setup: ```cpp pinMode(SD_CS, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(SD_CS, HIGH); ``` Then call SD.begin(SD_CS). 4) Reduce SD write frequency and/or write size Once per minute is already low, but ensure you’re not flushing excessively. Opening/writing/closing once per minute is fine. Avoid calling f.flush() multiple times. 5) Slow SPI clock (optional) Some SD modules + jumper wires are flaky at default SPI speed. The SD library uses a default; you can switch to SdFat library for explicit speed, but as a quick test you can shorten wires and add caps first. 6) If still flaky: power SD module from a separate 5V source (with common ground) or use a better 3.3V regulator Some SD modules have an onboard regulator that’s not great; powering from Uno 3.3V is often not enough current, so better is stable 5V into the module (as you do) plus caps. Also: BME280 on 5V VIN is probably fine if the board has a regulator. If it doesn’t have level shifting, I2C at 5V could be stressing it, but your symptom being tied to SD writes still screams power. A quick diagnostic you can code: - Read BME280 multiple times, before and after SD write, and print whether the read fails. ```cpp float t1 = bme.readTemperature(); logSample(...); float t2 = bme.readTemperature(); Serial.print("t1="); Serial.print(t1); Serial.print(" t2="); Serial.println(t2); ``` If t2 becomes NAN right after SD, that’s consistent with a transient. Try the capacitor first; it’s the most common fix. Tell me what values you have available.
I added a 330uF cap across SD VCC and GND and it’s much better, but I still get an occasional NAN. Could I add retries to the sensor read?

Yes—adding a simple retry wrapper is a good robustness layer, especially if occasional bus glitches remain. Approach: - Attempt to read each measurement a few times. - If a read returns NAN (or absurd values), wait a short time and try again. - If it still fails, keep the last known good value and log a marker. Example helper: ```cpp bool readBME_safe(float &tC, float &rh, float &p_hPa) { const uint8_t MAX_TRIES = 5; for (uint8_t i = 0; i < MAX_TRIES; i++) { float t = bme.readTemperature(); float h = bme.readHumidity(); float p = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; bool ok = !isnan(t) && !isnan(h) && !isnan(p) && (t > -40 && t < 85) && (h >= 0 && h <= 100) && (p > 300 && p < 1100); if (ok) { tC = t; rh = h; p_hPa = p; return true; } delay(10); // small pause before retry } return false; } ``` Then in your sampling: ```cpp static float lastT = NAN, lastH = NAN, lastP = NAN; float tC, rh, p_hPa; bool ok = readBME_safe(tC, rh, p_hPa); if (!ok) { // fall back tC = lastT; rh = lastH; p_hPa = lastP; } if (ok) { lastT = tC; lastH = rh; lastP = p_hPa; } ``` Logging: if not ok, you can log empty fields or a flag column: CSV idea: - Add a “ok” column: 1 for good read, 0 for fallback. Or log “nan” explicitly by printing a string, but that complicates numeric parsing. A flag column is easier. Also consider one more hardware tweak: - Add 0.1 µF near BME280 VCC/GND if you haven’t. - If you’re using a breadboard, sometimes the contact resistance causes tiny drops; moving to soldered connections helps. If you want, I can show a full updated loop that logs with an OK flag and updates the LCD with either live or last-known values.
Show me the full updated loop and logging function with the OK flag. Also I want the LCD to show “ERR” briefly when a read fails.

Here’s one way to implement it with minimal changes: a safe read function, a log function that includes an ok flag, and LCD behavior that flashes “ERR” for a short time when the read fails. Assumptions: - You already have `bme`, `lcd`, SD initialized. - Logging to "log.csv". - Sampling once per minute. Add these globals: ```cpp const uint32_t SAMPLE_MS = 60000UL; uint32_t lastSample = 0; static float lastT = NAN, lastH = NAN, lastP = NAN, lastAlt = NAN; float seaLevel_hPa = 1013.25; ``` Safe read: ```cpp bool readBME_safe(float &tC, float &rh, float &p_hPa, float &alt_m) { const uint8_t MAX_TRIES = 5; for (uint8_t i = 0; i < MAX_TRIES; i++) { float t = bme.readTemperature(); float h = bme.readHumidity(); float p = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; float a = bme.readAltitude(seaLevel_hPa); bool ok = !isnan(t) && !isnan(h) && !isnan(p) && !isnan(a) && (t > -40 && t < 85) && (h >= 0 && h <= 100) && (p > 300 && p < 1100); if (ok) { tC = t; rh = h; p_hPa = p; alt_m = a; return true; } delay(10); } return false; } ``` LCD update helpers: ```cpp void lcdShowReadings(float tC, float rh, float p_hPa) { lcd.setCursor(0,0); lcd.print("T:"); char buf[8]; dtostrf(tC, 5, 1, buf); // width 5, 1 decimal lcd.print(buf); lcd.print("C "); lcd.print("H:"); dtostrf(rh, 5, 0, buf); // width 5, 0 decimals lcd.print(buf); lcd.setCursor(0,1); lcd.print("P:"); dtostrf(p_hPa, 7, 1, buf); lcd.print(buf); lcd.print("hPa   "); // pad to clear leftovers } void lcdFlashErr() { // Put ERR at end of first line lcd.setCursor(13,0); lcd.print("ERR"); delay(300); lcd.setCursor(13,0); lcd.print("   "); } ``` Logging with OK flag: ```cpp void logSample(uint32_t ms, float tC, float rh, float p_hPa, float alt_m, uint8_t okFlag) { File f = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { f.print(ms); f.print(","); f.print(tC, 2); f.print(","); f.print(rh, 2); f.print(","); f.print(p_hPa, 2); f.print(","); f.print(alt_m, 2); f.print(","); f.println(okFlag); // 1=good, 0=fallback f.close(); } else { // optional: also show SD error Serial.println("Failed to open log.csv"); } } ``` Updated `loop()`: ```cpp void loop() { uint32_t now = millis(); if (now - lastSample >= SAMPLE_MS) { lastSample = now; float tC, rh, p_hPa, alt_m; bool ok = readBME_safe(tC, rh, p_hPa, alt_m); if (ok) { lastT = tC; lastH = rh; lastP = p_hPa; lastAlt = alt_m; } else { // fallback to last known values if available tC = lastT; rh = lastH; p_hPa = lastP; alt_m = lastAlt; lcdFlashErr(); } // If we still have NAN because no prior good sample, you can choose to skip logging uint8_t okFlag = ok ? 1 : 0; lcdShowReadings(tC, rh, p_hPa); logSample(now, tC, rh, p_hPa, alt_m, okFlag); Serial.print("Logged. ok="); Serial.println(okFlag); } } ``` Don’t forget to update your CSV header once (in setup) to include `ok`: - `ms,tempC,humRH,presshPa,altm,ok` If you want “ERR” to show without blocking delay, we can make it non-blocking with a timestamp instead of `delay(300)`, but for once-per-minute sampling the brief delay is usually fine.
I’d like to add a real-time clock so the CSV has actual date/time. I have a DS3231 module. How do I wire it and change the code?

DS3231 is also I2C, so wiring is simple and it can share SDA/SCL with the BME280 and LCD. Wiring (DS3231): - DS3231 VCC -> 5V (most modules) or 3.3V (many are fine on either; check silkscreen) - DS3231 GND -> GND - DS3231 SDA -> A4 - DS3231 SCL -> A5 I2C address: - DS3231 is typically 0x68. Library options: - RTClib (by Adafruit) is common and straightforward. Steps: 1) Install “RTClib” in Library Manager. 2) First run a “set time” sketch once to set the DS3231 to compile time. 3) Then integrate into your logger to timestamp each line. Set-time sketch (upload once): ```cpp #include <Wire.h> #include "RTClib.h" RTC_DS3231 rtc; void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); Wire.begin(); if (!rtc.begin()) { Serial.println("Couldn't find RTC"); while (1); } // Set RTC to the date & time this sketch was compiled: rtc.adjust(DateTime(F(__DATE__), F(__TIME__))); Serial.println("RTC set to compile time"); } void loop() {} ``` Integration changes: - In setup(), initialize rtc and check it. - In loop(), get `DateTime now = rtc.now();` - Format timestamp into CSV, e.g. `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS` Example timestamp print to File without building big Strings: ```cpp void printDateTimeCSV(File &f, const DateTime &dt) { char buf[20]; // "YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS" = 19 chars + null snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "%04d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d", dt.year(), dt.month(), dt.day(), dt.hour(), dt.minute(), dt.second()); f.print(buf); } ``` Update your log function signature to include DateTime: ```cpp #include "RTClib.h" RTC_DS3231 rtc; void logSample(const DateTime &dt, float tC, float rh, float p_hPa, float alt_m, uint8_t okFlag) { File f = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { printDateTimeCSV(f, dt); f.print(","); f.print(tC, 2); f.print(","); f.print(rh, 2); f.print(","); f.print(p_hPa, 2); f.print(","); f.print(alt_m, 2); f.print(","); f.println(okFlag); f.close(); } } ``` Then in loop: ```cpp DateTime dt = rtc.now(); logSample(dt, tC, rh, p_hPa, alt_m, okFlag); ``` Update header to: - `datetime,tempC,humRH,presshPa,altm,ok` One more I2C consideration: you now have 3 devices on I2C (BME, LCD, RTC). It’s fine, but keep wiring tidy and consider I2C pullups: you might have pullups on multiple modules, making the effective pullup too strong. Usually still okay at 5V/100kHz, but if you see weirdness, you can remove some pullups (often by desoldering resistors) or ensure bus is at 100k. If you want, I can provide a consolidated sketch that includes BME280 + LCD + SD + DS3231 with the retry logic.
Yes, give me the consolidated sketch. I’m okay if it’s long. Use 115200 baud. Include the header creation logic but don’t duplicate it every boot if the file exists.

You can check for file existence with `SD.exists()`. If it doesn’t exist, create it and write the header; otherwise, append. Consolidated sketch (BME280 + I2C LCD + SD + DS3231, with retries and OK flag). You may need to adjust LCD address (0x27) and SD CS pin (10).

Before you paste it, one more thing: I want the LCD to show time on the second line and temperature/humidity on the first line. Pressure can be shown briefly every 10 seconds by alternating the display.

I’ll implement: - Line 1 alternates every 10 seconds between: - Mode A: Temp + Hum - Mode B: Pressure (and maybe “hPa”) - Line 2 always shows current time (HH:MM:SS) and date in a compact way (e.g., MM/DD). - Logging still once per minute with full data. That’s a common mismatch: there are multiple LiquidCrystal_I2C libraries with slightly different APIs. Easiest fix is to adjust the LCD initialization to match your library. Common variants: 1) Some use: - `lcd.init();` - `lcd.backlight();` 2) Others use: - `lcd.begin(16, 2);` - `lcd.backlight();` or `lcd.setBacklight(HIGH);` If your error says `init()` not found and you know `begin()` exists, change: ```cpp lcd.init(); lcd.backlight(); ``` to: ```cpp lcd.begin(16, 2); lcd.backlight(); ``` If `backlight()` isn’t available, try: ```cpp lcd.setBacklight(HIGH); ``` Or sometimes: ```cpp lcd.backlight(); // works ``` If you tell me the exact library name as shown in the IDE (Sketch -> Include Library) and the constructor line you’re using, I can tailor it exactly. Also, I’m seeing “RTC not found” intermittently. Could it be an I2C pullup issue with all these modules?

Yes—intermittent I2C device detection with multiple modules can be caused by pullup strength, wiring capacitance, or borderline voltage levels. What’s happening electrically: - I2C requires pullup resistors on SDA/SCL. Many modules include 4.7k pullups to VCC. - If you have pullups on the LCD backpack, DS3231 module, and BME280 breakout, they end up in parallel. Three 4.7k in parallel is ~1.6k, which is a strong pullup (higher current). That can distort edges, cause ringing, and in some setups lead to bus issues. - Long wires and breadboards add capacitance; strong pullups can help with rise time but can also cause overshoot and noise. Practical steps: 1) Lower I2C speed to 100kHz explicitly: ```cpp Wire.begin(); Wire.setClock(100000); ``` If something set it to 400k, forcing 100k often stabilizes things. 2) Reduce pullups (ideally keep one set) - Pick one module to provide pullups, remove/disable the others. - Many boards have two resistors labeled R1/R2 or “472” (4.7k). You can desolder one pair on two of the modules. - If you don’t want to desolder, sometimes there’s a jumper to disconnect pullups (less common on cheap LCD backpacks, more common on some sensor breakouts). 3) Ensure consistent I2C voltage - If all modules are powered at 5V, SDA/SCL are pulled to 5V. DS3231 is 5V tolerant. LCD backpack is fine. BME280 depends on breakout; if it has level shifting, fine; if not, risky. - Intermittent “RTC not found” can also be the bus being held low by a device that’s unhappy. 4) Check for bus lockups and add recovery If a device glitches mid-transaction, it can hold SDA low. Some sketches add an I2C bus recovery routine that toggles SCL a few times to release the bus before reinitializing devices. 5) Wiring quality - Keep SDA/SCL short, run a ground reference alongside them. - Avoid loose jumpers. Quick diagnostic: - Run the I2C scanner repeatedly in a loop and see if 0x68 (RTC) disappears when SD writes occur. If yes, again power/noise is involved. - Add a 0.1 µF and maybe 10 µF capacitor near DS3231 VCC/GND too. If you want a software workaround: - On “RTC not found,” don’t halt; instead retry initialization and keep logging with millis timestamp until RTC returns. But the underlying bus stability should still be fixed. Tell me: are you powering everything from the Uno 5V pin, and are you using a breadboard with long jumper wires? That tends to correlate with these I2C issues.
Yes, everything is on jumper wires. I’d like an I2C recovery routine and also make the program not freeze if RTC init fails. It should keep running and try again later.

Add two things: 1) I2C bus recovery: if SDA is stuck low, pulse SCL up to 9 times to allow a slave to release SDA, then send a STOP. 2) Soft RTC handling: try rtc.begin(); if it fails, set a flag `rtcOk=false`. In loop, periodically retry (e.g., every 5 seconds). When rtcOk is false, display “NO RTC” and log with millis-based timestamp or leave datetime blank. I’ll outline the recovery code you can drop in. I2C recovery (Uno: SDA=A4, SCL=A5): ```cpp void i2cRecoverBus() { pinMode(A4, INPUT_PULLUP); // SDA pinMode(A5, INPUT_PULLUP); // SCL delay(2); // If SDA is low, try to clock it out if (digitalRead(A4) == LOW) { pinMode(A5, OUTPUT); for (int i = 0; i < 16; i++) { digitalWrite(A5, HIGH); delayMicroseconds(5); digitalWrite(A5, LOW); delayMicroseconds(5); if (digitalRead(A4) == HIGH) break; } } // Send STOP: SDA low->high while SCL high pinMode(A4, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(A4, LOW); delayMicroseconds(5); pinMode(A5, INPUT_PULLUP); // let SCL go high delayMicroseconds(5); pinMode(A4, INPUT_PULLUP); // release SDA high delayMicroseconds(5); // Restore Wire Wire.begin(); Wire.setClock(100000); } ``` Soft RTC init: - In setup: call a function `tryInitRTC()` that sets `rtcOk`. - In loop: if not ok and time since last try > RETRY_MS, call `i2cRecoverBus()` then try again. Pseudo: ```cpp bool rtcOk = false; uint32_t lastRtcTry = 0; const uint32_t RTC_RETRY_MS = 5000; void tryInitRTC() { rtcOk = rtc.begin(); } ``` When logging: - If rtcOk, use rtc.now(). - Else log millis time in a different column or a placeholder. Best CSV approach: include both `unix` or `datetime` and `ms`. But DS3231 doesn’t provide unix directly; RTClib can: `dt.unixtime()`. If you want minimal change: keep `datetime` column. If no RTC, print `0000-00-00 00:00:00` and okFlag plus an `rtcOk` column. Great. Now I want to calibrate seaLevel_hPa. I know my actual altitude is 162 meters. Can you add code to compute sea-level pressure from measured pressure and known altitude, and save it to EEPROM so it persists?

Use the barometric formula relationship used by the library: seaLevel = pressure / pow(1.0 - altitude/44330.0, 5.255) Where: - pressure is in hPa at your location - altitude is meters (your known altitude: 162 m) Then store seaLevel_hPa in EEPROM with a simple signature to validate. Implementation idea: - On boot: read EEPROM; if valid, use stored seaLevel_hPa, else default 1013.25. - Provide a “calibration mode” triggered by Serial command (e.g., send ‘C’) that: - reads current pressure (average a few samples) - computes seaLevel_hPa using known altitude - stores it to EEPROM - prints the new value Before I paste code: do you want calibration triggered via Serial (simple), or via a button connected to a pin? Serial is easiest.
Use Serial command. ‘C’ to calibrate, ‘P’ to print current stored sea-level value, and ‘R’ to reset to default. Add these commands without breaking the rest.

Add EEPROM support and a small serial command handler. Below are the additions/changes. 1) Add include at top: #include <EEPROM.h> 2) Add these constants/globals near seaLevel_hPa: const float DEFAULT_SEALEVEL_HPA = 1013.25; const float KNOWN_ALT_M = 162.0;   // your real altitude const uint16_t EEPROM_MAGIC = 0xBEE2; const int EEPROM_ADDR_MAGIC = 0;   // 2 bytes const int EEPROM_ADDR_SEA = 2;     // 4 bytes float float loadSeaLevelFromEEPROM() { uint16_t magic; EEPROM.get(EEPROM_ADDR_MAGIC, magic); if (magic != EEPROM_MAGIC) return DEFAULT_SEALEVEL_HPA; float val; EEPROM.get(EEPROM_ADDR_SEA, val); if (isnan(val) || val < 800.0 || val > 1100.0) return DEFAULT_SEALEVEL_HPA; return val; } void saveSeaLevelToEEPROM(float val) { EEPROM.put(EEPROM_ADDR_MAGIC, EEPROM_MAGIC); EEPROM.put(EEPROM_ADDR_SEA, val); } void resetSeaLevelEEPROM() { // write magic wrong to invalidate uint16_t magic = 0x0000; EEPROM.put(EEPROM_ADDR_MAGIC, magic); } float computeSeaLevelFrom(float pressure_hPa, float altitude_m) { // seaLevel = pressure / pow(1 - alt/44330, 5.255) float term = 1.0 - (altitude_m / 44330.0); if (term <= 0) return DEFAULT_SEALEVEL_HPA; return pressure_hPa / pow(term, 5.255); } 3) In setup(), after you set seaLevel_hPa initially (or replace your existing initialization): seaLevel_hPa = loadSeaLevelFromEEPROM(); Serial.print("seaLevel_hPa="); Serial.println(seaLevel_hPa, 2); 4) Add a function to average pressure for calibration: bool readPressureAvg_hPa(float &pAvg) { const uint8_t N = 10; float sum = 0; uint8_t count = 0; My Ender 3 prints have bad stringing between features. What retraction settings should I try?

To dial in stringing on an Ender 3, I need a few specifics because “good” retraction depends on the extruder type, filament, temperature, and slicer. Quick starting point if you want something immediately: for a stock Bowden Ender 3, try retraction distance 5.0 mm and retraction speed 45 mm/s, with nozzle temp on the low end of your filament’s range and travel speed 150–200 mm/s. Before we iterate, tell me: 1) Is your Ender 3 stock Bowden (extruder on the frame) or direct drive? 2) Filament type (PLA/PETG/TPU) and brand if you know it 3) Nozzle size and print temp 4) Slicer (Cura/PrusaSlicer/OrcaSlicer) and your current retraction distance/speed 5) Do you see stringing only between separate parts (travel moves) or also oozing at corners?
It’s mostly stock Ender 3 V2, Bowden. PLA, Hatchbox. 0.4 nozzle. Printing around 205C. Cura. Retraction is 6.5mm at 25mm/s. Travel speed 150. Strings mostly between separate pillars in a test print.

Good, that’s enough to start. Your distance (6.5 mm) is on the higher side for an Ender 3 Bowden, and your speed (25 mm/s) is on the low side. Excessive distance can sometimes worsen stringing by pulling molten filament too far back, causing pressure inconsistencies, and it can also increase the chance of clogs if heat creep is present. Low speed can allow more time for ooze during the retract/unretract cycle. Try this structured approach: Step 1: Temperature sanity check - Hatchbox PLA at 205°C is reasonable, but if stringing is prominent, try 200°C or even 195°C if layer adhesion remains okay. - Don’t change multiple variables at once initially; however, temp is a big driver of ooze. Step 2: Retraction baseline for Bowden Ender 3 In Cura: - Retraction Distance: 5.0 mm - Retraction Speed: 40 mm/s (or 45 mm/s if your extruder can handle it without grinding) - Retraction Minimum Travel: 1.5 mm (or 1.0 mm) - Combing Mode: Within Infill (or “Not in Skin” depending on Cura version) - Z Hop: Off initially (Z hop can increase stringing due to extra dwell time, though it can reduce scars) - Wipe: If available, enable “Retract at Layer Change” and consider “Coasting” OFF initially (coasting can hide issues but complicates tuning) Step 3: Travel and acceleration - Travel speed 150 mm/s is fine. If you can push to 180–200 mm/s without ringing issues, it can reduce time spent oozing on travels. - If your printer’s acceleration is low, the effective travel might still be slower, but we’ll focus on retraction first. Step 4: Run a retraction tower/test Use a two-pillar stringing test (like the one you’re doing) and adjust: - If strings remain: increase retraction speed first (up to 45–50 mm/s), then small distance increments (5.0 → 5.5 → 6.0). - If you get under-extrusion right after travel or clicking: reduce distance or speed. One more question: is your Bowden tube seated properly against the nozzle (no gap), and do you have a good pneumatic coupler? A tiny hotend gap can cause ooze and inconsistent pressure, making stringing impossible to fully fix in software.
I haven’t checked the Bowden tube seating recently. Couplers seem okay but the tube can wiggle a bit at the hotend fitting. Also I’m using the stock plastic extruder arm.

That wiggle at the hotend coupler is a big red flag. If the tube can move in and out even slightly, retractions become “springy”: the first part of retraction just compresses/relaxes the tube instead of pulling filament, and during unretract it can push molten filament unpredictably. That often shows up as persistent strings no matter what settings you try. I’d do a quick mechanical check before burning time on slicer tuning: Hotend/Bowden seating check (Ender 3 style hotend) 1) Heat nozzle to ~200°C (PLA temp) so you can safely tighten without stripping cold plastic. 2) Remove filament. 3) Loosen the nozzle about 1/2 turn (while hot). 4) Push Bowden tube down firmly so it bottoms out against the nozzle/heatbreak. 5) Tighten the nozzle back up (while hot) so the nozzle seats against the heatbreak and the tube is trapped with no gap. 6) Let cool. Coupler fix: - If the hotend pneumatic fitting is worn, replace it (PC4-M6 on many Creality hotends, but confirm). - Add a small printed “Bowden clip” or a zip-tie style lock to prevent the coupler collar from lifting. Extruder arm: - The stock plastic extruder arm is notorious for cracking underneath where you can’t easily see. That can cause inconsistent extrusion and worsen stringing. - If you can, upgrade to a metal extruder (cheap and effective) or at least inspect the arm carefully. After you do that, retest with the baseline retraction settings (5.0 mm, 40 mm/s). Do you want a checklist for diagnosing whether you have a hotend gap (like seeing blobs, leaking at the heater block, inconsistent extrusion after retractions)?
Yes give me the checklist. I also sometimes see a little gunk on the outside of the nozzle after a print, like it’s oozed and then burned.

Checklist for hotend gap / leakage / retraction inconsistency: A) Signs of a Bowden-to-nozzle gap (internal leak) - Random blobs/zits even when seams are aligned - “Popping” or sizzling sounds (moist filament can also do this) - Under-extrusion right after retractions, then it recovers - Strings that persist across a wide range of retraction settings - Filament dust around extruder gear from repeated retract/unretract grinding B) Signs of external leak (nozzle/heater block threads) - Brown/black “gunk” on the outside of nozzle or heater block - Filament residue accumulating on the heater block, sometimes smoking - A shiny wet look around the top of the heater block where the nozzle threads in - The silicone sock gets gooey or stuck with plastic C) Quick checks (no disassembly needed) 1) With the printer hot (careful), look at the heater block: any plastic seepage from the top of the block or around nozzle threads? 2) Check if the silicone sock is hiding buildup—remove sock after cooling and inspect. 3) Do a small extrusion (like 10–20 mm) and watch: does plastic curl oddly from the nozzle due to a dirty tip? (Dirty nozzle can create strings by dragging.) D) Mechanical fixes - Hot-tighten nozzle properly: heat to printing temp, then snug the nozzle. Don’t overtighten; just firm. - Ensure the heatbreak is seated correctly. On Ender-style hotends, the nozzle should tighten against the heatbreak, not bottom out on the heater block. - Replace nozzle if it’s worn or if the tip is deformed. - Replace Bowden coupler if there’s any play. - Consider Capricorn tube if your stock tube is chewed up; but correct seating matters more than tube brand. E) Cleaning routine - Brass brush (gently) while hot to remove burnt plastic. - “Cold pull” with nylon or cleaning filament if you suspect debris inside. PLA can work in a pinch but nylon is better. Given you see burnt gunk on the nozzle, I’d prioritize: remove sock, inspect for leaks, then do the hot-tighten + Bowden reseat method. Once the hotend is sealed, retraction tuning becomes much more predictable. If you do that, tell me: does the residue appear to come from above the nozzle threads (near the heater block) or just from the nozzle tip picking up stray ooze?
It seems mostly on the nozzle tip and a little on the bottom of the heater block, not like seeping from the top. I haven’t removed the sock before.

That sounds more like normal “nozzle booger” buildup from small ooze + wiping across prints, but it’s still worth checking under the sock because it can hide a slow leak. Here’s a safe way to inspect and clean: 1) Power off and cool completely. 2) Remove the silicone sock carefully. 3) Look for plastic above the heater block seam (where the heatbreak goes in). If it’s clean up there, good. 4) Heat to 200°C. 5) Use tweezers to peel off softened plastic from the block/nozzle exterior (avoid yanking heater wires). 6) Lightly brush the nozzle with a brass brush. Avoid steel brushes; they can scratch and also short the heater leads if you’re careless. Then do a quick “pressure test” print: - Print a simple cube with 2 walls, 0% infill, no top, at your normal settings. - Watch for any new plastic appearing around the heater block. Once that’s done, go back to stringing. Since your current settings are 6.5 mm @ 25 mm/s, I’d change to: - 5.0 mm @ 45 mm/s - 200°C nozzle - Travel 180 mm/s if your printer handles it In Cura, also check: - “Retraction Extra Prime Amount” should be 0. - “Enable Retraction” obviously on. - “Maximum Retraction Count” and “Minimum Extrusion Distance Window” (if set too low/high) can cause odd behavior. Defaults usually fine. Do you have “Combing Mode” enabled? If combing is set to “All”, Cura might avoid retractions by routing travel moves through infill, which can sometimes reduce stringing but can also create wisps in open areas depending on geometry.
Combing is set to “All”. Z hop is enabled at 0.2mm because I was trying to avoid scratches. Coasting is on (0.064mm3). Wipe is off. Also I see little hairs even inside cavities sometimes.

Those settings explain a lot. Z hop increases the time spent during retraction events (lift, travel, lower), and coasting can reduce pressure at the end of lines but can also create inconsistent pressure recovery, sometimes making fine hairs worse. Combing “All” can reduce retractions but may route travels across areas where oozed filament gets dragged into cavities. For a clean baseline, I’d temporarily simplify: Baseline “stringing diagnosis” profile (Cura) - Combing Mode: Within Infill (or Not in Skin) - Z Hop When Retracted: OFF (for now) - Coasting: OFF (for now) - Wipe: If available as “Wipe Nozzle Between Layers” ignore; but “Wipe while Retracting” (in some Cura versions) can be ON later - Retraction Distance: 5.0 mm - Retraction Speed: 45 mm/s - Retraction Minimum Travel: 1.0–1.5 mm - Print Temp: 200°C - Fan: 100% after first layers (for PLA) - Flow: 100% (don’t change yet) Then run your two-pillar test and also a “stringing torture test” that includes retractions over gaps and inside holes. After baseline, add back Z hop only if you truly need it: - If scratches are due to over-extrusion or curling corners, fixing cooling/flow or enabling “Avoid printed parts when traveling” might solve it without Z hop. - If you must use Z hop, keep it small (0.2 is fine) but expect you may need slightly higher retraction speed or slightly lower temp to compensate. About hairs inside cavities: that’s often either too-hot PLA, moist filament, or retraction not triggering because minimum travel is too high or combing avoids retracts. Can you share your Cura “Retraction Minimum Travel” and “Maximum Retraction Count”? And how old/dry is the PLA (has it been sitting out)?

Minimum travel is 1.5mm. Max retraction count is 100. Minimum extrusion distance window is 10mm. Filament has been out for months, no dryer. I do hear occasional popping on some prints but not constantly.

Months out + occasional popping strongly suggests moisture is contributing. Wet PLA can string badly because steam bubbles in the melt cause oozing and wispy “hair.” Retraction tuning helps, but drying often makes the biggest immediate difference. What I’d do in parallel: 1) Dry the filament - Ideal: filament dryer at ~45–50°C for 4–6 hours for PLA. - Budget method: food dehydrator works well. - If you use an oven, be careful—many ovens overshoot and can soften/spoil the spool. If you do it, verify with an accurate thermometer and keep it low. 2) Baseline retraction profile changes (keep min travel 1.5mm, that’s fine) - Set combing to “Within Infill” - Disable Z hop and coasting - 5.0 mm @ 45 mm/s - 200°C, fan 100% 3) Add “Avoid printed parts when traveling” This can reduce nozzle dragging without Z hop. It may add travel time, so watch for stringing; but with good retraction it’s usually fine. 4) If popping persists even after drying, check nozzle contamination A partially clogged nozzle can cause pressure fluctuations and stringing. Your max retraction count/window settings look okay and unlikely to be limiting (100 is high). The important thing is that retraction is actually happening on those travels; combing “All” might be suppressing it. If you can’t dry right away, still try the baseline slicer settings; but expect improvement will be limited if the filament is wet. Do you want a quick way to confirm moisture without special tools (visual cues during extrusion, surface finish, weight changes)?
Sure. Also, when I manually extrude filament in the air it sometimes comes out with tiny bubbles and looks rough, not a smooth strand.

That rough, bubbly extrusion is a classic moisture indicator. Quick moisture confirmation methods: 1) Extrude test: With nozzle at 200–210°C, extrude 50–100 mm into the air. - Dry PLA: smooth, glossy strand, minimal popping. - Wet PLA: audible pops, tiny steam puffs, strand looks foamy/rough, occasional sideways spurts. 2) Surface finish: Wet PLA often prints with a slightly matte, “fuzzy” look and random tiny pits. 3) Stringing character: Wet filament tends to create very fine angel-hair strings everywhere, not just between pillars. 4) Bend test (not definitive): Some wet PLA becomes more brittle, snapping easily, but PLA can be brittle anyway depending on formulation. Given you’re seeing bubbles, I’d treat drying as step zero. You can still tune retraction, but you’ll be chasing a moving target. Once you dry it, rerun the stringing test with: - 200°C - 5.0 mm retract - 45 mm/s retract speed - combing within infill - coasting off - z hop off If you still get strings after drying, then we fine-tune: - Try 4.5 mm or 5.5 mm distance depending on symptoms - Try 40 vs 50 mm/s - Try lowering temp to 195°C One more mechanical thing: check your Bowden tube ends. If the filament path has extra friction (tube worn, sharp cut), retraction becomes less effective. Is your tube end cut square and fully seated? And do you have the stock hotend fan running reliably (heat creep can also worsen stringing)?

Hotend fan seems fine, always on. Tube end at extruder is kind of chewed where the collet bites it. Hotend end I haven’t pulled out. I can dry filament in a food dehydrator. What temp and how long exactly for a 1kg spool?

For PLA in a food dehydrator: - Temperature: 45°C is safe; 50°C is usually still safe for most spools but watch that the spool doesn’t soften/warp (some spools deform around 55–60°C). - Time: 4–6 hours is a good starting point for a 1 kg spool that’s been out for months. If it’s very wet, 8 hours won’t hurt. - Airflow matters: dehydrators work well because they circulate air. Practical tips: - Start at 45°C for 6 hours. If your dehydrator runs hot or isn’t well-controlled, 45°C reduces risk. - After drying, store the spool in a sealed bag/bin with desiccant. - If you can, print directly from a dry box or keep it covered between prints. While filament dries, also fix the chewed tube end: - Trim 5–10 mm off the end cleanly with a sharp cutter so it’s square. - If the tube is short after trimming, consider replacing the Bowden tube. A fresh tube + good couplers makes retraction more consistent. After drying and trimming, run the same stringing test. If you can, note the humidity in your area, but even without that, you should see the bubbly extrusion improve significantly. When you’re ready, tell me the results of: 1) Extrude-in-air test after drying (smooth vs bubbly) 2) Stringing test result with baseline settings

I dried it at 45C for 6 hours. Extrusion is much smoother now, no popping. I tried your baseline: 200C, 5mm at 45mm/s, combing within infill, coasting off, z hop off. Stringing is way better but still small wisps between pillars. Not thick strings, just fine hair.

That’s the expected progression: drying eliminates the worst of it, leaving only the normal fine “angel hair” that you can typically remove with minor tuning. For fine wisps, the usual levers (in order) are: slightly lower temp, slightly faster travel, and small retraction adjustments. Try this next set, one change at a time: Test A: Lower temp - Keep everything else the same, set nozzle to 195°C. If adhesion and extrusion are still good, this often removes the last wisps. If Test A helps but not enough: Test B: Increase travel speed - Keep 200°C (or 195°C if that worked) and raise travel speed to 180–200 mm/s. This reduces time for ooze during travel. If still wisps: Test C: Slightly increase retraction speed - 5.0 mm, 50 mm/s Watch your extruder for skipping/grinding; if it starts clicking, back down. If still: Test D: Small distance tweak - Try 5.5 mm at 45 mm/s If you see under-extrusion after retractions, go back. Also check Cura’s “Retraction Prime Speed” (sometimes separate): - Set prime speed similar to retract or slightly lower, like 35–45 mm/s, to avoid overshoot. Finally, enable “Wipe while retracting” if your Cura has it. Wiping can reduce the little thread left on the nozzle at the start of travel. Do you have “Outer Wall Wipe Distance” or “Wipe while Retracting” available in your Cura version? And what are your print speeds (outer wall, inner wall)?

Cura has “Wipe Nozzle Between Layers” but not sure about wipe while retracting. Outer wall speed 25, inner 50, infill 60. Retraction prime speed is same as retraction speed I think. Travel 150 currently. I can bump travel.

“Wipe Nozzle Between Layers” is a different feature (it often parks and wipes on a tower/brush if configured), so ignore that for now. Given your relatively slow outer wall speed (25 mm/s), the nozzle spends more time hot and pressurized near the end of a feature, which can contribute to slight ooze before travel. But that’s normal; we’ll handle it with temp/travel. Do this sequence: 1) Keep your print speeds as-is for now. 2) Set travel speed to 200 mm/s. 3) Drop temp to 195°C. Run the same pillar test. If you still have wisps: 4) Increase retraction speed to 50 mm/s (keep distance 5.0). 5) Optionally set “Minimum Travel” to 0.8–1.0 mm so retraction triggers more often, but don’t go too low if it causes excessive retractions on tiny moves. A note: Fine hairs can also come from nozzle tip contamination. After a few prints, wipe the nozzle while hot with a folded paper towel held by tweezers (careful) or use a brass brush. A clean nozzle tip releases less filament during travel. After you test 195°C + 200 mm/s travel, tell me: - Does it eliminate the wisps entirely? - Any signs of under-extrusion or weaker layer bonding?

At 195C and 200 travel, the wisps are almost gone. There’s still a couple tiny hairs but much better. Layer adhesion seems okay. But I noticed occasional little gaps right after a travel move, like the line starts a bit thin then recovers.

That “thin start after travel” is a sign your retraction/prime behavior is slightly under-priming or the filament pressure isn’t recovering instantly. Since you’re already getting low stringing, we want to fix the start gaps without bringing strings back. Options, in a safe order: 1) Reduce retraction distance slightly - Try 4.5 mm (keep 45 mm/s), still at 195°C and travel 200. Less distance means less chance of pulling molten filament back too far, so pressure recovers faster. 2) Adjust “Retraction Prime Speed” (if separate) - If Cura allows: set Prime Speed a bit slower than retract, e.g. retract 45, prime 35–40. This can reduce “snap” and inconsistent restart. (Depending on extruder, sometimes faster prime helps; but on Bowden, too aggressive prime can overshoot and blob.) 3) Add a tiny “Extra Prime Amount” only if needed - I prefer to keep Extra Prime at 0, but if the thin start persists after distance tweak, you can try +0.02 to +0.05 mm³ (very small). Too much causes blobs at the start. 4) Check “Coasting” very carefully - Coasting can cause thin ends/starts. You already turned it off; keep it off for now. Given your symptom, I’d try retraction distance 4.5 mm, same speed. Run the pillar test AND a simple perimeter-only print (like a single-wall cube) to see if the seam area looks consistent. Also, check if “Retract at Layer Change” is enabled. Sometimes disabling it reduces seam artifacts, but it can increase zits; it’s geometry dependent. Do you have “Linear Advance” (Marlin K-factor) enabled on your firmware? Many stock Ender 3 setups don’t, but if you do, it affects pressure behavior a lot.

I don’t think I have linear advance. Stock firmware. Retract at layer change is enabled. I tried 4.5mm and it helped the thin starts. Stringing came back a tiny bit but still acceptable. Is there a way to keep 5mm but fix the thin start?

Yes—if you want to keep 5.0 mm for stringing control but improve restart, you can tune prime behavior rather than distance. Try these: A) Keep distance 5.0 mm, speed 45 mm/s, but increase “Retraction Minimum Travel” slightly This sounds counterintuitive, but if you’re retracting on very short travels, you can get lots of retract cycles that amplify pressure lag. However, your issue is right after travels between pillars (long travel), so this may not be it. B) Prime speed tweak (most likely) If Cura exposes “Retraction Prime Speed” separately: - Retraction Speed: 45 mm/s - Prime Speed: 50 mm/s This can refill the nozzle faster and reduce the thin start. Watch for blobs at the restart; if blobs appear, reduce to 45 or 40. If Cura does not expose it, it uses the same speed for both. C) “Extra Prime Amount” tiny nudge If prime speed is not adjustable, use a very small extra prime: - Extra Prime Amount: start with +0.02 mm³ If still thin, +0.04 mm³. Do not jump high; it’s easy to overshoot. D) Enable “Outer Wall Wipe Distance” (if available) Some Cura builds have “Outer Wall Wipe Distance.” A small wipe (0.2–0.4 mm) can reduce the string left on the nozzle before travel without increasing retraction, and it can make restarts cleaner. E) Mechanical: reduce Bowden slop Even with good settings, Bowden systems have elastic lag. Tight couplers, a good tube, and minimizing tube length all help. Since your tube was chewed, replacing it entirely could reduce pressure lag and let you use 5 mm without thin starts. Since 4.5 mm already helped, a hybrid approach often works best: - Retraction distance 4.8 mm (middle ground) - Retraction speed 45 mm/s - Temp 195°C - Travel 200 mm/s That often keeps stringing low and restarts clean. Can you tell me whether your Cura has “Retraction Extra Prime Amount” visible, and whether it’s currently 0?
Yes it’s visible and set to 0. Prime speed not separate. Outer wall wipe distance is available but set to 0.0. I can try extra prime. I’m worried about blobs.

Then do it in tiny increments and verify on a seam-sensitive print. Suggested test: 1) Set Retraction Distance back to 5.0 mm. 2) Keep 45 mm/s, 195°C, travel 200. 3) Set Outer Wall Wipe Distance to 0.2 mm (small). 4) Set Extra Prime Amount to +0.02 mm³. Run: - Your two-pillar stringing test (to ensure stringing doesn’t return) - A simple cube with a visible seam (to check for blobs) Interpretation: - If thin starts are fixed and no blobs: keep it. - If blobs at restart: reduce extra prime back to 0 and rely on wipe only, or drop extra prime to +0.01 if Cura allows. - If thin starts persist: increase extra prime to +0.04 mm³, but watch closely for blobs. Also, “Outer Wall Wipe Distance” can slightly reduce the need for extra prime by managing pressure at the end of the line. It’s generally safer than extra prime for avoiding blobs, but it can create tiny under-extrusion at the end of walls if too high. Keep it small (0.2–0.4). If this still doesn’t fully solve it, the next most effective improvement is mechanical: replace the Bowden tube and couplers so retraction is crisp and repeatable. Do you want recommendations on which Bowden tube/couplers are worth getting and how to install them without introducing a hotend gap?
Yes. Also I’ve heard Capricorn tubing helps. Is it actually useful for PLA stringing?

For PLA stringing specifically, Capricorn tubing can help a bit, but it’s not magic. The real benefits are: - tighter inner diameter (less filament slop) - often better quality/consistency than stock tubes This improves retraction responsiveness and reduces hysteresis, which can reduce both stringing and restart gaps. What to buy (practical, common parts): 1) Bowden tube - Capricorn XS (1.9 mm ID) is the common “tight” PTFE tube. - Any good-quality PTFE tube with consistent ID can work, but Capricorn is a known baseline. 2) Pneumatic couplers - For the extruder: PC4-M10 (often; depends on extruder) - For the hotend: PC4-M6 (often) Creality sometimes uses those, but verify the thread size by looking up Ender 3 V2 hotend coupler spec or checking your current fittings. 3) Clips/locks - Small C-clips that prevent the coupler collar from being pulled up. These reduce tube movement, which is critical. Installation tips (avoid hotend gap): - Cut tube ends perfectly square. Use a tube cutter or a sharp razor in a guide; angled cuts cause leaks/gaps. - Hotend side: use the heat-and-seat method: 1) Heat to ~200°C 2) Loosen nozzle a half turn 3) Push tube down firmly 4) Tighten nozzle - Make sure the tube cannot move up/down in the coupler. Use the clip. Extruder side: - Insert tube fully, then lock the collar with a clip. - Check that the extruder fitting isn’t worn; if it’s the original and the tube was chewed, replace it. Does Capricorn help for PLA stringing? - It can reduce the retraction distance needed by ~0.5–1.0 mm in some setups because the system is less “spongy.” - It can make retractions more consistent across many retracts. - But temperature, moisture, and slicer behavior still dominate. Since drying and temp already helped you a lot, tubing is an incremental upgrade, not a primary fix. If you install Capricorn and good couplers, you may be able to use: - Retraction distance 4.0–4.5 mm instead of 5.0 - Same or slightly lower extra prime (maybe back to 0) Do you have the stock plastic extruder still? If so, a metal extruder upgrade also improves consistency (less flex), which can reduce stringing and improve retraction response.
Yes stock plastic extruder. I’m considering a metal one. But I’m worried about changing too much and then having to re-calibrate everything. What changes would require recalibration?

Good instinct—changing multiple hardware pieces at once can make it hard to know what fixed what. Here’s what typically requires recalibration and what doesn’t: A) Metal extruder (same style, still Bowden) Likely recalibration needed: - E-steps (extruder steps/mm): very likely, because the drive gear diameter and grip differ. - Flow/extrusion multiplier: maybe a small tweak after E-steps, but often not necessary if E-steps are correct. Retraction settings: may need minor adjustment because extrusion becomes more consistent. B) Bowden tube replacement (Capricorn) Recalibration: - Usually no E-step change just from tube. - Retraction distance/speed may change (often can be reduced slightly). - Temperature usually unchanged. C) Nozzle replacement (same size) Recalibration: - Usually no E-step change. - Flow might change slightly if the old nozzle was worn or partially clogged. - Retraction might improve if the old nozzle tip was rough/dirty. D) Direct drive conversion Recalibration: - Almost everything: retraction distance becomes much smaller (0.6–1.5 mm typical), speeds change, accelerations may need tuning. Not recommended if you want minimal disruption. If you want minimal upheaval, do upgrades in this order: 1) Replace couplers + Bowden tube (low risk, mostly improves consistency) 2) Metal extruder (then calibrate E-steps once) 3) Optional: new nozzle E-steps calibration isn’t too painful: - Mark filament, command 100 mm extrusion, measure actual, compute new E-steps. But it does require sending an M92/M500 or changing firmware settings depending on how you control it. Do you have a way to send G-code commands (OctoPrint, Pronterface, Cura terminal, or the printer’s screen menus)? Ender 3 V2 stock firmware sometimes hides E-step adjustment in the UI depending on version.
I use Cura over SD card, no OctoPrint. The printer menu has “Steps/mm” under motion, I think. I can adjust E steps there. How do I do the E-steps calibration properly?

E-steps calibration procedure (safe, standard, Bowden setup): Prep 1) Make sure the hotend is at printing temperature for the filament (PLA: 200°C is fine). Cold extrusion will be blocked. 2) Remove the Bowden tube from the hotend side if you want the most accurate measurement without nozzle backpressure. However, that measures “free air” extrusion, which can differ slightly under load. Many people do it through the nozzle; both are acceptable. I’ll give the through-nozzle method since it’s simpler and realistic. Through-nozzle method (common) 1) Load PLA and ensure it’s flowing normally. 2) Using calipers/ruler, measure 120 mm up from where filament enters the extruder (the point right before it goes into the extruder). Put a clear mark at 120 mm. 3) On the printer, command an extrusion of 100 mm at a slow rate (like 5 mm/s or whatever the menu uses; slower is better to avoid skipping). 4) After extrusion finishes, measure the distance from the extruder entry point to your mark. - If the mark is now 20 mm away: perfect (extruded exactly 100 mm). - If it’s 25 mm away: you extruded only 95 mm (under-extruded). - If it’s 15 mm away: you extruded 105 mm (over-extruded). Compute new E-steps New_Esteps = Current_Esteps * (Commanded_Extrusion / Actual_Extrusion) Example: - Current E-steps: 93.0 - Commanded: 100 mm - Actual: 95 mm New = 93.0 * (100/95) = 97.89 Apply and save - Set the new E-steps in the menu (Steps/mm for E). - Save settings (store to EEPROM if available). Verify - Repeat the 100 mm test again. Aim for within 1 mm. Important notes - Make sure you’re not extruding too fast; skipping will ruin the measurement. - Do not calibrate E-steps by changing flow in the slicer. E-steps is the machine’s truth; flow is fine tuning for filament/nozzle. After E-steps, do a quick single-wall cube to check line width and flow, but don’t chase perfection immediately—stringing tests first. If you install a metal extruder, do this calibration once afterward. If you keep the same motor and just change the arm, the E-step change may be small but still worth verifying. Do you know your current E-steps value (often around 93 for Creality)?
It’s 93.0 right now. I’ll probably do the metal extruder and Capricorn tube together. Is that a bad idea?

It’s manageable if you’re methodical, but doing both at once means if something goes wrong (new stringing, under-extrusion), you’ll have two potential causes. If you’re okay with a little extra troubleshooting, it’s fine. To reduce risk: - Install both, then immediately calibrate E-steps. - Don’t change slicer settings at the same time. Keep your current “mostly working” profile (195°C, travel 200, retraction 5 mm @ 45) so you can see the hardware effect. Post-upgrade checklist: 1) Verify smooth filament path: no sharp bends, tube fully seated and clipped. 2) Verify extruder tension: not too tight (grinds filament) and not too loose (slips). With a metal extruder, the spring tension may differ. 3) Calibrate E-steps. 4) Print a simple extrusion test: a straight line skirt/brim and watch for consistent flow. 5) Re-run stringing test; then tweak retraction distance downward if needed (Capricorn often lets you reduce to 4–4.5 mm). One more thing: when changing the extruder, make sure the drive gear is aligned with the filament path and the set screw is tight on the motor shaft flat. A slightly slipping gear can cause inconsistent extrusion that looks like stringing/ooze issues. If you want, tell me which metal extruder kit you’re considering (single-gear MK8 style, dual-gear, etc.). Some dual-gear extruders change the E-steps more dramatically and can behave differently.
It’s a cheap dual-gear extruder kit from Amazon, says “BMG style clone” but still Bowden. Should I avoid that?

A BMG-style dual-gear clone can work well, but there are a few caveats: Pros - Better grip on filament (less slipping) - More consistent extrusion, especially with retractions - Can reduce grinding and improve print quality Caveats / what changes 1) Gear ratio: Many BMG-style extruders are geared (commonly 3:1). That means E-steps will change a lot (often into the 400+ steps/mm range depending on motor/gear). - If your kit is truly geared, you must recalibrate E-steps; 93 will be wildly wrong. 2) Build quality varies - Some clones have poorly machined gears, misalignment, or too-tight bearings. - If gears aren’t aligned, filament can get chewed, creating dust and inconsistent retractions. 3) Firmware max feedrate / step rate - With very high E-steps, the firmware may hit step rate limits at high extrusion speeds, but for typical Ender 3 PLA speeds it’s usually okay. - Retraction speed in mm/s becomes more demanding in steps/s. You may need to lower retraction speed slightly if the extruder motor sounds strained. If you want minimum fuss, a simple metal MK8-style (same geometry) is easiest. If you’re okay calibrating and possibly dialing in motor current/tension, BMG-style can be a nice upgrade. How to tell if it’s geared: - If it has a big plastic/metal gear housing and a small drive gear inside, likely geared. - If the filament path goes between two hobbed gears and the motor is offset with gears, it’s geared. - Product listing often says “3:1”. If it is geared, a common starting E-steps is around 415 steps/mm, but don’t trust that—calibrate. Also note: Retraction distance may be able to drop slightly because extrusion is more precise, but Bowden tube still dominates distance. Do you know if the listing says 3:1 or gives a recommended E-step value?
It says 3:1 and recommends E steps around 415. Okay. I’ll install it. After that, what retraction settings should I start with?

With a 3:1 BMG-style extruder but still Bowden, start close to what already worked, then adjust: Starting point (after E-steps calibration) - Retraction Distance: 4.5 mm - Retraction Speed: 35–40 mm/s initially Why lower speed? Geared extruders can handle it, but the step rate is higher; starting slightly lower avoids missed steps/noise. You can increase later. Keep the other known-good settings: - Temp: 195°C (for your Hatchbox PLA, since it reduced wisps) - Travel: 200 mm/s - Combing: Within Infill - Coasting: Off - Z hop: Off (add later only if needed) - Extra Prime: start at 0 again after hardware change; don’t carry over compensations until you retest - Outer Wall Wipe Distance: 0.2 mm is fine to keep Then run: 1) E-steps calibration 2) Stringing test 3) A retraction distance tower from 3.5 to 6.0 mm (since Bowden still needs distance) 4) Check for restart gaps; if present, consider tiny extra prime again (+0.02 mm³) If you see grinding or the motor “chirps” during retractions, reduce retraction speed. After you install, tell me: - Final calibrated E-steps - Whether the extruder is noisy during retracts - Stringing vs restart quality at the starting 4.5 mm

Installed it and Capricorn tube. Calibrated E steps: ended up at 418.6 for 100mm. It’s a bit noisier on retractions but not skipping. Using 4.5mm at 40mm/s, 195C, travel 200. Stringing is basically gone on the pillar test. But now I’m getting occasional little blobs on the surface, like zits, especially near seams.

Nice progress—stringing solved means your retraction/moisture/temp path is solid. Now we’re in “surface artifact” territory. Seam zits/blobs are typically caused by pressure release/restart behavior, and Cura’s seam placement plus retraction at layer change can accentuate them. Things to try (in order, minimal disruption): 1) Seam settings - Set Z Seam Alignment to “Sharpest Corner” or “Back” (depending on model), so blobs are hidden in a corner rather than scattered. - If it’s already in one place and still blobby, we tune pressure. 2) Retraction at layer change - Try disabling “Retract at Layer Change.” This can reduce the “extra ooze” at the seam because the printer won’t do an extra retract/prime cycle at every layer transition. It can slightly increase stringing in some cases, but since your stringing is basically gone, you may be fine. 3) Reduce Extra Prime (keep at 0) You said extra prime is 0—good. Keep it there while diagnosing. 4) Outer Wall Wipe Distance tweak - If wipe is 0.2 mm, try increasing slightly to 0.4 mm. Wipe reduces pressure at the end of the line and can reduce seam blobs. If you go too high you can get thin wall ends; keep it modest. 5) Retraction distance micro-adjust Sometimes blobs come from too little retraction (ooze at stop/start). You’re at 4.5 mm. Try 4.8 mm. But note: more retraction can also create restart artifacts if prime overshoots, so do small changes. 6) Temperature - If you can print at 190°C with good adhesion, that can reduce oozing and seam blobs further. But don’t go so low you get poor layer bonding. Also check for these Cura items: - “Print Cooling”: ensure fan is high; poor cooling can make blobs more visible. - “Minimum Layer Time”: if it slows down small layers, it can ooze at the seam. If your zits happen on small features, this is relevant. When you say blobs: are they exactly at the seam line (one vertical line), or are they scattered randomly across the surface?
Mostly at the seam line, one vertical line, but sometimes I see a random zit elsewhere. Also I have “Minimum Layer Time” set to 10s and “Minimum Speed” 10mm/s.

Seam-line blobs: that’s very consistent with layer change behavior + pressure. Random zits elsewhere can come from: - slight over-extrusion - filament contamination - temperature fluctuations - or Cura doing tiny retractions/starts due to combing/pathing Let’s focus on seam first. Try this seam-blob reduction set: 1) Disable “Retract at Layer Change.” 2) Increase Outer Wall Wipe Distance from 0.2 to 0.4 mm. 3) Keep retraction 4.5 mm @ 40 mm/s, 195°C. Then print a simple cylinder or cube with smooth outer wall to evaluate the seam. About Minimum Layer Time: - On small layers, Cura will slow down to meet 10s, which increases time for ooze at the seam. If your model has small cross-sections (like a tower), seam blobs get worse. - You can reduce Minimum Layer Time to 5s or keep 10s but increase Minimum Speed to 15–20 mm/s so it doesn’t crawl. However, cooling may suffer on tiny parts. It’s a trade. For the occasional random zit: - Check “Combing Mode”: Within Infill should reduce unnecessary retractions on outer walls, but pathing can still cause micro-stops. - Also check if “Power Loss Recovery” is enabled on the printer; some printers pause briefly to write to SD, causing a tiny blob. On some Creality setups, this can create periodic zits. If you notice zits at regular vertical intervals, that’s a clue. Do your random zits appear at consistent heights (like every few millimeters), or truly random?
Now that you mention it, I think they are kind of periodic, like every 8mm or so up the print. Not perfectly, but there’s a pattern.

Periodic zits strongly suggest something like SD write latency / power-loss recovery logging, or sometimes Z-axis binding—but zits rather than layer shifts points more to brief pauses. Common Ender 3 V2 culprit: “Power Loss Recovery” (sometimes called “Resume Print” feature). It periodically writes to the SD card; if the SD card is slow, the printer pauses momentarily, leaving a blob. What to do: 1) Try a different SD card - Use a name-brand, smaller capacity card (8–32 GB) formatted FAT32. - Avoid very old or very large cards. 2) Disable power loss recovery if possible - Some firmwares have a menu option to turn it off. - If not, some custom firmwares (like Jyers/Professional) let you disable it, but that’s a bigger change. 3) Print from USB/OctoPrint (optional) - Not required, but it eliminates SD write pauses. You said you’re SD-only, so try the SD card swap first. How to confirm: - Print a tall smooth cylinder and watch carefully: if you see the printer hesitate briefly at intervals, that’s it. - The zits will line up around the model at those heights. Meanwhile, the seam blobs are slicer-level; do the “disable retract at layer change + wipe 0.4” test too. Do you know what SD card you’re using (brand/size), and is it the one that came with the printer?
Yes it’s the cheap SD card that came with it. 8GB no-name. I’ll try a SanDisk. About power loss recovery, I don’t see an option in menus.

Swapping that card is one of the highest ROI fixes for periodic blobs on Creality printers. The included cards are notorious for inconsistent write performance. Do this: 1) Get a SanDisk (or Samsung) 16GB or 32GB microSD, with adapter if needed. 2) Format FAT32 (default allocation is usually fine). 3) Copy only the G-code you need (don’t clutter it with hundreds of files). 4) Print the same model and see if the periodic zits disappear. If they persist even with a good card, then power-loss recovery writes may still cause a pause, but a better card often makes the pause so short it doesn’t blob. If you later decide to change firmware, you can disable recovery, but try the card first. For seam blobs, after you change: - Retract at layer change OFF - Wipe 0.4 If seam blobs reduce but you start seeing occasional stringing at layer changes, you can re-enable retract at layer change but reduce “Z Seam” visibility by aligning it to a corner and using wipe. One more factor: “Minimum Layer Time” slowing can worsen seam blobs. If your test object is a narrow tower, keep that in mind. For diagnosing seam behavior, use a model with reasonable layer time (like a 40 mm cube). Let me know results after the SD card swap and the seam setting changes.

New SanDisk card helped a lot. The periodic zits are basically gone. Seam blobs improved after disabling retract at layer change and wipe 0.4, but the seam is still a little raised. Any way to make it smoother?

A slightly raised seam is common; you can usually reduce it further with a combination of seam placement, pressure management, and sometimes linear advance (but you’re stock firmware). Staying within slicer tweaks: 1) Seam placement strategy - If the seam is “aligned” and you see a ridge, try “Random” seam to distribute it. That can make it less noticeable overall, though it can add tiny marks everywhere. - Alternatively, “Sharpest Corner” hides it best on models with corners. 2) Flow tuning (small) A raised seam can indicate slight over-extrusion or extra pressure at restarts. - Try reducing Flow to 98% (global) for PLA. Do not go too low; you’ll get gaps. But 98% often smooths surfaces. 3) Retraction and wipe fine-tuning - Wipe 0.4 is already helping. You can try 0.5–0.6 mm, but watch for thinning at the end of wall lines. - Retraction distance: try 4.7 mm (from 4.5) to reduce ooze right before stopping—sometimes that reduces seam ridge. If it causes thin starts, revert. 4) “Coasting” (carefully, small) You previously turned off coasting for tuning. Now that stringing is solved, a tiny amount of coasting can reduce seam blobs by relieving pressure before the end of a line. - If you try it: start extremely small (since Cura coasting is in volume): - Coasting Volume: 0.02–0.04 mm³ Too much will cause gaps at line ends. 5) Wall ordering - Print “Outer Wall Before Inner Wall” OFF (so inner walls first, outer last) is typical for best surface. But sometimes swapping can affect seam. If you have it, try toggling and compare. Usually outer last is better. 6) Speed at outer wall - If outer wall is very slow (25), the nozzle can ooze slightly more at the seam. Try 30–35 mm/s outer wall if quality is still good. Faster outer wall can reduce dwell and seam bulge. Don’t go crazy; you’ll trade it for ringing. Given you’re close, the least risky combo is: - Flow 98% - Keep wipe 0.4 - Try outer wall speed 30 - Keep retract 4.5 mm If seam still raised, then try tiny coasting 0.03 mm³. Do you have a photo of the seam ridge? Also, what layer height are you printing (0.2?) and what line width?
Layer height 0.2, line width 0.4. No photo right now. I’ll try flow 98 and outer wall 30. One more question: is there any benefit to enabling “Combing: Not in Skin” instead of Within Infill?

Yes—“Not in Skin” (in Cura terms) tells the slicer to avoid traveling across top/bottom surfaces (skin) without retracting, which can reduce scars and wisps on visible surfaces. “Within Infill” confines combing to infill regions when possible, which also helps. Which is better depends on the model: - Within Infill: generally good for reducing travel marks on outer surfaces, and it encourages retractions when moving across perimeters. - Not in Skin: specifically protects top/bottom surfaces; it can increase retractions compared to “All,” but often improves cosmetic quality. If you’re chasing surface perfection and your retractions are now solid (and you’ve got a geared extruder), “Not in Skin” is often a good choice. It can slightly increase print time and retraction count, but that’s usually fine for PLA. So: yes, try “Not in Skin” if you see travel marks on top surfaces or inside cavities. If you see no difference, keep “Within Infill.” Since your stringing is under control, extra retractions are less risky now—just keep an eye on filament grinding (should be less with BMG-style) and hotend heat creep (unlikely at PLA with good fan). After you test flow 98% and outer wall 30, if the seam ridge is still prominent, your next best knob is tiny coasting or slightly adjusting retraction distance (4.6–4.8). If you want a “final” tuned profile summary for your current hardware (Ender 3 V2 Bowden + Capricorn + BMG clone), I can consolidate what we’ve found.
Yes consolidate the profile. And include any settings I should avoid changing casually.
